dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for unconscious patient at the given address.
Hi, I'm Mark, an EMT with the rescue team. What made you call 911 today?
She collapsed when she tried to get up from the bed and hasn't responded since. I called 911 right away.
Is she responsive at all-eye opening or motor movement?
No, her eyes are wide open, but she's not moving or reacting.
Are there any vocalizations or responses to loud spoken commands?
No verbal or movement response. I've been trying without success.
Is her airway intact and patent?
At first, she was not breathing at all-she was apneic. I tried to stimulate her but didn't get a response.
Partner, check respirations and signs of respiratory distress.
She had 5 shallow breaths per minute initially, but now it's 14. Still some distress.
No clear radial pulse at first, but a weak carotid pulse was palpable. She's still in a respiratory arrest state.
Patient is cool, clammy, and pale. No edema noted. Prepare for IV access attempt.
First blood pressure reading is 85 over 58-low for her age.
Blood glucose is 455-hyperglycemic, no need to treat for low sugar.
Have you seen any other symptoms like cough, diarrhea, or fever?
No cough or fever that I saw. Just vomiting for two days and extreme tiredness.
What medications does she regularly take?
Lantus insulin for her diabetes, and she takes something for high blood pressure.
Any other health issues we should know about?
She has CHF, high blood pressure, and a history of seizures from a while ago.
Can you describe exactly what happened when she collapsed?
She was getting out of bed and then fell over. No head injury, no signs of a recent seizure.
Apply 4-lead ECG since she shows altered mental status with possible neurological causes.
ECG attached. She is in sinus rhythm, no ST elevation, ectopy, or dysrhythmias.
She has a pain score of 0. No signs of acute distress or discomfort at this time.
Her skin is pale and clammy with no swelling, rashes, or bite marks.
GCS is 3. She is unresponsive to voice, pain, and eye opening.
Her history included possible seizures, but no current signs of active one.
She was near her bed when I found her. She appeared to roll off and then stopped.
Blood pressure improved to 109/69. She is trending more stable.
No signs of shock now. Keep monitoring closely.
Her oxygen saturation is at 90%. Considering supplemental O2.
12-lead EKG complete. Still sinus rhythm with nonspecific T-wave abnormality.
Temperature is 98.4-no fever, no signs of infection.
Breathing rate now is 14-better but still shallow and rapid.
Current blood pressure is 75/40-still low, but signs of improvement beginning.
Attempt IV access but if unsuccessful and pressure still improving, avoid IO.
IV in left arm with 20-gauge failed. We will monitor BP instead.
Protocol 3-15 for altered mental status is in use.
Agreed. She remains under medical protocol for monitoring and transport.
Let's check again. Any changes in breathing, GCS, or stable signs.
Breathing 9 per minute now-slower, more controlled.
Still weak. Heart rate is 82 with better oxygen saturation of 99%.
Blood pressure reads 109/69 now. Looks better.
SpO2 is now 99%-good increase from earlier.
No immediate drug treatment. Blood sugar too high to address right now.
Still warm, clammy, and pale. No signs of trauma.
Respiratory rate has improved to 10 per minute, breathing more stable.
No signs of respiratory distress now. Even breathing sounds at both lungs.
GCS improved to 9. She is reacting to verbal stimuli and eye tracking.
BP is now normal 109/69, and no pain current. Respirations steady.
Rate is 12 RPM-normalizing.
Skin remains clammy but no swelling or trauma to worry about.
GCS at 9. She can now track and follow basic verbal instructions.
Hospital of choice is [COMPANY_NAME].
Patient loaded onto reeves stretcher and strapped securely.
Our four-person team is moving her down carefully with a four-point carry.
She is now on the ambulance stretcher and secured for transport.
[COMPANY_NAME] is on standby and has been informed of the condition.
ER team has been advised. We are now prepared for handoff.
Vitals continue improving and GCS at 9. Transport conditions good.
SpO2 is now at 97%. Stable and progressing favorably.
BP at 115/72, pulse 83, and rhythm regular.
EtCO2 monitoring applied. Square waveform observed within normal range.
All vitals trending well-no deterioration or new issues.
[COMPANY_NAME] wants her assessed quickly. Prefer ER to be ready.
Next EKG read: heart in sinus rhythm. No arrhythmias.
Making contact with [COMPANY_NAME]. They are preparing for arrival.
They are ready. Sharing vitals and key protocol information now.
BP 121/68 with 100% SpO2. All stable for transport.
GCS now at 13. She can follow commands and respond verbally.
No signs of swelling, edema, bleeding, or trauma. No pain detected.
[COMPANY_NAME] is ready for handover. Final preparations begin.
Personal medical history and current status is being shared.
I can accompany the team or bring over the patient's things if needed.
Suspected history of a minor seizure episode. Patient is stable with GCS improving.
[COMPANY_NAME], we are en route with a post-ictal patient in fair condition.
We have you on standby. Prepare for arrival in bed 10.
Patient unit is approaching ER. Bed 10 is allocated.
All medical documentation updated. [COMPANY_NAME] has completed transfer acceptance.
Transfer complete. All systems confirm patient is in their care.
Bed 10 is ready. Assessing patient and reviewing transport notes.
Full transfer followed. [COMPANY_NAME] team has assumed full patient care.
She is placed in bed 10 via sheet move. Ecosystem stable.
Transition complete. We are beginning full evaluation.
[COMPANY_NAME] has received the case. Clear for M193 unit return.
All transfer steps followed with appropriate documentation completed.
Current vitals are 109/71 BP and 99% SpO2. Stable for next steps.
Last EKG read before exit showing sinus rhythm with no emergent findings.
Patient is post-ictal and stable for full ER care management.
All work done here. [COMPANY_NAME] team has patient in full control.
ER lead assumed. Transport is now successfully concluded.
Respiratory is 15 RPM, volume and depth now stable with endurance.
Full transition and safe transfer. All steps complete as of now.
